Introduction to Python Project : FoodHub Data Analysis¶

Problem statement¶

Background¶

The number of restaurants in New York is increasing day by day. Lots of students and busy professionals rely on those restaurants due to their hectic lifestyles. Online food delivery service is a great option for them. It provides them with good food from their favorite restaurants. A food aggregator company FoodHub offers access to multiple restaurants through a single smartphone app.

The app allows restaurants to receive a direct online order from a customer. The app assigns a delivery person from the company to pick up the order after it is confirmed by the restaurant. The delivery person then uses the map to reach the restaurant and waits for the food package. Once the food package is handed over to the delivery person, he/she confirms the pick-up in the app and travels to the customer's location to deliver the food. The delivery person confirms the drop-off in the app after delivering the food package to the customer. The customer can rate the order in the app. The food aggregator earns money by collecting a fixed margin on the delivery order from the restaurants.

Objective¶

The food aggregator company has stored the data of the different orders made by the registered customers in their online portal. They want to analyze the data to get a fair idea about the demand of different restaurants which will help them in enhancing their customer experience.

Data Dictionary¶

The data includes various information related to a food order. A detailed data dictionary is provided below.

  • order_id: Unique ID of the order
  • customer_id: ID of the customer who ordered the food
  • restaurant_name: Name of the restaurant
  • cuisine_type: Cuisine ordered by the customer
  • cost_of_the_order: Cost of the order
  • day_of_the_week: Indicates whether the order is placed on a weekday or weekend (The weekday is from Monday to Friday and the weekend is Saturday and Sunday)
  • rating: Rating given by the customer out of 5
  • food_preparation_time: Time (in minutes) taken by the restaurant to prepare the food. This is calculated by taking the difference between the timestamps of the restaurant's order confirmation and the delivery person's pick-up confirmation.
  • delivery_time: Time (in minutes) taken by the delivery person to deliver the food package. This is calculated by taking the difference between the timestamps of the delivery person's pick-up confirmation and drop-off information

Let us start by importing the required libraries¶

Note : Data loaded from google colab files and not from google drive

In [202]:
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
pd.set_option('max_colwidth', None)

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

Understanding the structure of the data¶

orders = pd.read_csv('/content/sample_data/foodhub_order.csv')

Initial Observations¶

  • Verified : The order dataset belonging to FoodHub has been loaded properly & head() returns 5 rows. Dataset has the same 9 colums as provided in the Data Dictionary

  • Numerical Columns : These 6 columns order_id, customer_id, cost_of_the_order, rating, food_preparation_time, delivery_time have numerical data.

    • Among these, cost_of_the_order has data of float nature and the rest are integers

  • Text Columns: These 3 columns restaurant_name, cuisine_type, day_of_the_week have text data.

    • Among these cuisine_type, day_of_the_week have text of categorical nature and restaurant_name has strings that are expected to be pronouns

Question 2: What are the datatypes of the different columns in the dataset? (The info() function can be used) [0.5 mark]¶

In [207]:
# Write your code here
data.info()

<class 'pandas.core.frame.DataFrame'>
RangeIndex: 1898 entries, 0 to 1897
Data columns (total 9 columns):
 #   Column                 Non-Null Count  Dtype  
---  ------                 --------------  -----  
 0   order_id               1898 non-null   int64  
 1   customer_id            1898 non-null   int64  
 2   restaurant_name        1898 non-null   object 
 3   cuisine_type           1898 non-null   object 
 4   cost_of_the_order      1898 non-null   float64
 5   day_of_the_week        1898 non-null   object 
 6   rating                 1898 non-null   object 
 7   food_preparation_time  1898 non-null   int64  
 8   delivery_time          1898 non-null   int64  
dtypes: float64(1), int64(4), object(4)
memory usage: 133.6+ KB

Observations:¶

  • Total 9 columns are present

  • There are 5 numerical columns : order_id, customer_id, cost_of_the_order, food_preparation_time

    • Among these cost_of_the_order is declared as float64
    • Rest 4 columns are int64

  • There are 4 object columns : restaurant_name, cuisine_type, day_of_the_week, rating

  • Rating column

    • rating column captures numerical value and should have been a integer type but is declared as object
    • Non rated values are present in this column but are captured as this text Not given

  • Nulls

    • All columns are marked as non-null and hence should not be nulls in any of the column.

Question 3: Are there any missing values in the data? If yes, treat them using an appropriate method. [1 mark]¶

In [208]:
# Write your code here

print("\nMissing values per column:\n", data.isnull().sum())
print("-"*10)
print('\nRating count by rating value\n', data['rating'].value_counts())

Missing values per column:

customer_id              0
restaurant_name          0
cuisine_type             0

day_of_the_week          0

delivery_time            0
dtype: int64
----------

Rating count by rating value
 rating
Not given    736
5            588
4            386
3            188
Name: count, dtype: int64

Observations:¶

  • Non Nulls

    • All columns are marked as non-null and hence there are no null values in any of the column.

  • Rating column

    • Non rated values are present in rating column.
    • They are captured Not given
Missing data treatment¶
  • rating column is converted to int64 from object
  • The 'Not givenstring value is replaced withNaN`. Conversion is done first to float and then to int64 to preserve the NaN
  • Conversion is stored back in the same rating column
  • Following code converts and verifies
In [209]:
data['rating'] = data['rating'].replace('Not given', np.nan)
data['rating'] = data['rating'].astype(float).astype('Int64')

print("\n Data Info\n", data.info())
print("-"*10)
print('\nRating count by rating value\n', data['rating'].value_counts(dropna=False))

<class 'pandas.core.frame.DataFrame'>

 5   day_of_the_week        1898 non-null   object 
 6   rating                 1162 non-null   Int64  
 7   food_preparation_time  1898 non-null   int64

Imputing Rating data - considerations & next steps¶
  • Since the rating column is categorical and also ordinal, filling the missing values with median is an option
  • If the mean of the rating is close the median, imputing with mean is also an option
  • We can also study the correlation of ratings with other columns cost, preparation time and delivery time
  • Also a high percentage (38.7%) data has no rating
In [210]:
print("\nMean (Rating) : ", data['rating'].mean())
print("Median (Rating) : ", data['rating'].median())
print("Mode (Rating) : ", data['rating'].mode())

correlation = data[['delivery_time', 'food_preparation_time','rating', 'cost_of_the_order']].corr()
correlation

Mean (Rating) :  4.344234079173838
Median (Rating) :  5.0
Mode (Rating) :  0    5
Name: rating, dtype: Int64
Out[210]:
delivery_time food_preparation_time rating cost_of_the_order
delivery_time 1.000000 0.011094 -0.009804 -0.029949
food_preparation_time 0.011094 1.000000 -0.006083 0.041527
rating -0.009804 -0.006083 1.000000 0.033983
cost_of_the_order -0.029949 0.041527 0.033983 1.000000

Observations and Imputing next steps:

  • No ratings for 1 and 2 shows people rarely have given low ratings.
  • Data is highly skewed towards 5 and 4 i.e., Median is 5 and Mean is 4.34
  • Filling with Mean (4.34) is not a good choice as ratings are discrete (1,2,3, 4,5) and also the distribution is not normal
  • There is nearly no correlation to cost, delivery time or preparation time.

Hence, it is decided to use the simple approach of imputing with 5 in a new column 'new_rating'.

In [211]:
data['new_rating'] = data['rating'].copy()
data['new_rating'] = data['new_rating'].fillna(5)
print(data[['rating', 'new_rating']].head())

   rating  new_rating
0    <NA>           5
1    <NA>           5
2       5           5
3       3           3
4       4           4

Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)¶

Univariate Analysis¶

Question 6: Explore all the variables and provide observations on their distributions. (Generally, histograms, boxplots, countplots, etc. are used for univariate exploration.) [9 marks]¶

Exploring the numerical variables in following order

  1. cost_of_the_order
  2. food_preparation_time
  3. delivery_time
  4. rating
  5. customer_id

Exploring the non-numerical variables in following order

  1. restaurant_name
  2. cuisine_type
  3. day_of_the_week

[Univariate 1] Observations on Cost of the order¶

In [214]:
# Write the code here
bins = [i for i in range(0, 55, 5)]
plt.xlim(0,50)
sns.histplot(data=data, x = 'cost_of_the_order', kde=True, bins = bins)
plt.xlabel('Cost of order')
plt.ylabel('Frequency')
plt.show()

sns.boxplot(data=data, x = 'cost_of_the_order')
plt.xlabel('Cost of order')
plt.show()
  • Distribution of cost is slightlyt skewed to the right
  • There are no outliers on either side (observed from the boxplot)
  • Cost range $10 to $15 has the highest number of orders
  • 50% of the orders fall in the $10 to $20 range
  • Order count has decreased as the cost went high (This correlation can be analysed more in Multivariate)
  • Presence of multiple groups of cost ranges are visiblein the kde

[Univariate 2] Observations on Food preparation time¶

In [215]:
# Write the code here

## basic histogram with KDE
bins = [i for i in range(18, 40, 1)]
xlabel = 'Food preparation time'
plt.xlim(15,40)
plt.xlabel(xlabel)
plt.ylabel('Count of Orders')
sns.histplot(data=data, x = 'food_preparation_time', kde=True, bins = bins)
plt.show()

## Box plot
sns.boxplot(data=data, x = 'food_preparation_time')
plt.xlabel(xlabel)
plt.show()

## violin plot
sns.violinplot(x=data['food_preparation_time'])
plt.show()

## advanced histogram with bins in the range of 15-20, 20-25, 25-30, 30-35 Mins
bins = list(range(15, 40, 5))
labels = [f"{bins[i]}-{bins[i+1]} mins" for i in range(len(bins)-1)]

data['prep_time_bins'] = pd.cut(data['food_preparation_time'], bins=bins,
                                labels=labels, right=False)

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
sns.histplot(data=data, x='prep_time_bins', stat="percent", discrete=True)

plt.xlabel("Food Preparation Time (in 5 min buckets)")
plt.ylabel("Percentage of Orders %")
plt.show()

3 charts are generated for the food preparation time data

  1. Histogram (basic and advanced with 5 min buckets)
  2. Boxplot
  3. Violin plot

Observations

  • Distribution of food preparation time is uniform. This is evident from the histograms and other charts
  • There are no outliers on either side (observed from the boxplot)
  • Every min (in the 20 to 35 min range) food is prepared for 100 or more orders

[Univariate 3] Observations on Delivery time¶

In [216]:
# Write the code here

## basic histogram with KDE
bins = [i for i in range(12, 40, 1)]
xlabel = 'Delivery time'
plt.xlim(15,40)
plt.xlabel(xlabel)
plt.ylabel('Count of Orders')
sns.histplot(data=data, x = 'delivery_time', kde=True, bins = bins)
plt.show()

## Box plot
sns.boxplot(data=data, x = 'delivery_time')
plt.xlabel(xlabel)
plt.show()

## violin plot
sns.violinplot(x=data['delivery_time'])
plt.show()

## advanced histogram with bins in the range of 15-20, 20-25, 25-30, 30-35 Mins
bins = list(range(15, 40, 5))
labels = [f"{bins[i]}-{bins[i+1]} mins" for i in range(len(bins)-1)]

data['delivery_time_bins'] = pd.cut(data['delivery_time'], bins=bins,
                                labels=labels, right=False)

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
sns.histplot(data=data, x='delivery_time_bins', stat="percent", discrete=True)

plt.xlabel("Delivery Time (in 5 min buckets)")
plt.ylabel("Percent of Orders %")
plt.show()

# Write the code here
order_cost_gt_20 = (data['delivery_time'] > 30).sum()
order_percent = (order_cost_gt_20/len(data)) * 100
print(f"Percentage of orders costing more than $20: {order_percent:.2f}%")

Percentage of orders costing more than $20: 7.85%

3 charts are generated for the delivery time data

  1. Histogram (basic and advanced with 5 min buckets)
  2. Boxplot
  3. Violin plot

Observations

  • Distribution of delivery time is left skewed
  • There are no outliers plotted on either side of the boxplot
  • Nearly 50% of the orders are delivered in 25 Mins
  • Highest percent of orders are delivered in the 25 - 30 Min range, when a 5min window is taken

[Univariate 4] Observations on Rating¶

In [217]:
# Write the code here

## boxplot
sns.boxplot(x = data['new_rating'])
plt.xlabel('Rating')
plt.show()

sns.boxplot(x = data['rating'])
plt.xlabel('Rating (after imputing)')
plt.show()

## violin plot
sns.violinplot(x=data['new_rating'])
plt.xlabel('Rating (after imputing)')
plt.show()

## Bar graph
sns.countplot(data=data, x='new_rating');
plt.xlabel('Rating (after imputing)')
plt.show()

Observations:

  • Rating is left skewed. This is verified with provided data and imputed data. i.e., High number of Orders are rated '5'
  • No orders are rated below 3 (i.e., no rating for 1 and 2). This could be an issue in the data capturing aspect also

[Univariate 5] Observations on Customer¶

In [218]:
# Histogram of Customers and count of Orders placed
order_counts = data['customer_id'].value_counts()
bins = [i for i in range(0, 15, 1)]
sns.histplot(order_counts, bins=bins, kde=True)
plt.xlabel("Number of Orders per Customer")
plt.ylabel("Frequency")
plt.title("Distribution of Orders per Customer")
plt.show()

print("-" * 10)

# Boxplot of Customers and count of Orders placed
sns.boxplot(x=order_counts)
plt.xlabel("Number of Orders per Customer")
plt.title("Order Distribution per Customer")
plt.show()

# Unique customer count
num_unique_customers = data['customer_id'].nunique()
print("\nNumber of unique customers:", num_unique_customers)

# top 10 customers
print("\nTop 10 customers")
top_customers = order_counts.head(10)
print(top_customers)
print("\nTotal orders from top 10 customers", top_customers.sum())

Observations

  • There are 1200 unique customers
  • Average order count from a single customer is between 1 and 2
  • Orders by customer is right skewed
    • There are outliers among customers who have placed orders above 2, with 13 orders being the highest from a single customer
    • Top 10 highest ordering customers have placed 80 orders

Multivariate Analysis¶

Question 12: Perform a multivariate analysis to explore relationships between the important variables in the dataset. (It is a good idea to explore relations between numerical variables as well as relations between numerical and categorical variables) [10 marks]¶

Exploring the relationship between the following variables using multivariate analysis

  1. Customer & cost_of_the_order
  2. Customer & cuisine_type & cost_of_the_order
  3. Cuisine_type & cost_of_the_order
  4. Cuisine_type & food_preparation_time, delivery_time
  5. Order count by day of the week and cuisine type
  6. Average cost_of_the_order & day_of_the_week and cuisine type
  7. Top Restaurants and total time by day of the week
  8. Restaurant_name & total_cost_of_the_order (aggregate)
  9. Average 'total time' & day_of_the_week
  10. Rating, Restaurant and Cuisine type
  11. Day_of_the_week & rating
  12. Correlation between rating, total time, cost of the order

[Multivariate 1] Customer and Cost of the order¶

Lets check the total cost of order by customer

In [227]:
customer_spending = data.groupby("customer_id")["cost_of_the_order"].agg(["count", "sum", "mean"])
customer_spending.rename(columns={"count": "order_count", "sum": "total_spent", "mean": "avg_order_value"}, inplace=True)

print(customer_spending['avg_order_value'].describe().T)
print("-"*10)

sns.scatterplot(data=customer_spending, x="order_count", y="total_spent")
plt.title("Order Count vs. Total Spend per Customer")
plt.show()

print("-"*10)
orders_cost_by_count = customer_spending.groupby('order_count',  as_index=False)['total_spent'].sum()
orders_cost_by_count['percentage'] = (orders_cost_by_count['total_spent'] / orders_cost_by_count['total_spent'].sum()) * 100

plt.title('Order count vs Total Revenue Percentage (%)')
plt.ylabel('Revenue Percentage')
sns.barplot(data=orders_cost_by_count, x='order_count', y='percentage', legend=False)
plt.show()

count    1200.000000
mean       16.456487
std         6.591341
min         4.750000
25%        12.130000
50%        15.040000
75%        20.785000
max        33.320000
Name: avg_order_value, dtype: float64
----------

----------

Observations

  • There are 1200 unique customers
  • 59% or orders are from repeat customers
  • Average spending (i.e., cost_of_the_order) by a customer per order ranges between $16.45 to $33.32
  • Scatter plot shows the outlier customers with high spend.
    • Customer 52832 has spent $225 on 13 orders
  • There are 14 customers who have spent more than $100 on the orders

[Multivariate 2] Customer, cuisine type, cost of the order¶

In [228]:
customer_cuisine_spending = data.groupby(["customer_id", "cuisine_type"])["cost_of_the_order"].agg(["sum", "mean"]).reset_index()
rev_by_cuisine = customer_cuisine_spending.groupby("cuisine_type", as_index=False)['sum'].sum().sort_values(by='sum', ascending=False)
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
plt.title("Total Revenue by Cuisine Type")
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
sns.barplot(data=rev_by_cuisine, x='cuisine_type', y='sum')
plt.show()

Observations

  • Total spending by individual customers is high on American cuisine, followed by Japanese, Italian and Chinese

[Multivariate 3] Cuisine and cost of the order¶

In [229]:
plt.figure(figsize=(15,8))
sns.boxplot(data=data, x='cuisine_type', y='cost_of_the_order')
plt.title("Cuisine type vs. Cost of the order ")
plt.xticks(rotation=90);
plt.show()

print("-" * 10)

plt.figure(figsize=(25, 7))
plt.title("Cuisine type vs. Cost of the order ")
sns.swarmplot(data = data, x = "cuisine_type", y = "cost_of_the_order", size = 1)
plt.xticks(rotation=90);
plt.show()

----------

Observations:

  • Average cost of the order is high on French and Thai cuisines, around $20, inferred from the Box plot
  • Average cost of the 4 top preferred cuisines (American, Japanese, Italian and Chinese is comparable and below $15. High number of orders are concentrated around this and is visible from scatter plot

[Multivariate 4] Cuisine & Food preparation time, delivery time¶

In [230]:
# Melt the DataFrame to make it suitable for a grouped bar chart
data_avg = data.groupby("cuisine_type")[["food_preparation_time", "delivery_time"]].mean().reset_index()
data_melted = data_avg.melt(id_vars=["cuisine_type"], value_vars=["food_preparation_time", "delivery_time"],
                    var_name="Time_Type", value_name="Minutes")

# Plot the grouped bar chart
plt.figure(figsize=(16, 8))
sns.barplot(data=data_melted, x="cuisine_type", y="Minutes", hue="Time_Type", palette="coolwarm")

# Customize the chart
plt.title("Food Preparation vs Delivery Time by Cuisine")
plt.xlabel("Cuisine Type")
plt.ylabel("Time (Minutes)")
plt.legend(title="Time Type")
plt.show()

Observations :

  • Average delivery times are lesser than average food preparation times across all cuisines except for Vietnamese
  • Top selling cuisines (American, Japanese, Italian, Chinese) have comparable delivery and food preparation times respectively.

[Multivariate 5] Order count by day of the week and cuisine type¶

In [231]:
plt.figure(figsize=(16, 8))
plt.title("Order Count by vs Day of the week by Cuisine")
plt.ylabel("Order count")
plt.xlabel("Day of the week by Cuisine type")
sns.countplot(data=data, x='cuisine_type', hue='day_of_the_week');
plt.show()

Observations:

  • Across all cuisines, significantly High number of orders are received on Weekends
  • On Weekends as well as week days, Top selling cuisines are American, Japanese Italian, Chinese

[Multivariate 6] Average cost of the order by day of the week and cuisine type¶

In [232]:
plt.figure(figsize=(16, 8))
average_data = data.groupby(["day_of_the_week", "cuisine_type"])["cost_of_the_order"].mean().reset_index()
sns.swarmplot(data = average_data, x = "cuisine_type", y = "cost_of_the_order", hue='day_of_the_week', size=10)
plt.xticks(rotation=90);

Observations:

  • For American cuisine, the average cost per order increases during the weekends compared to other high selling cuisines Chinese, Italian & Japanese.
  • For Japanese cuisines, the average cost spend per order on weekends and weekdays are almost similar
  • Both these observations are leading to higher revenues on weekends as the order count is higher on weekends for these cuisines (observed earlier)

[Multivariate 7] Top Restaurants and total time by day of the week¶

In [233]:
data["total_time"] = data["food_preparation_time"] + data["delivery_time"]

top_restaurants = (
    data.groupby(["restaurant_name"])
    .size()
    .reset_index(name="order_count")
    .sort_values(by="order_count", ascending=False)
    .head(20)
)

filtered_data = data[data["restaurant_name"].isin(top_restaurants["restaurant_name"])]

plt.figure(figsize=(20, 10))
sns.scatterplot(data=filtered_data, x="restaurant_name", y="total_time", hue="day_of_the_week", palette="coolwarm", s=100)

# Customize
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.title("Total Time (Food Prep + Delivery) by Restaurant with Day of the Week")
plt.xlabel("Restaurant Name")
plt.ylabel("Total Time (Minutes)")
plt.legend(title="Day of the Week", bbox_to_anchor=(1.05, 1), loc="upper left")
plt.show()

Observations:

  • For the top 20 restaurants with highest order count, the total time taken (food prep time + delivery time) for most of the orders is high on week days compared to weekend orders

  • Average total time taken i.e., the sum of order preparation and delivery is higher on weekdays compared to weekeends

In [190]:
data_grouped_by_rest = data[data['new_rating'] <= 3].groupby('restaurant_name', as_index=False)['cuisine_type'].value_counts().sort_values(by='count', ascending=False).head(20)
plt.figure(figsize=(20, 8))
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.title("Ratings <= 3  by Top 20 Restaurants and Cuisines")
plt.xlabel("Restaurant Name")
plt.ylabel("Count of Rating <= 3")
sns.barplot(data=data_grouped_by_rest, x='restaurant_name', y='count', hue='cuisine_type')
plt.show()

Observations

  • Shake Shak restaurant of American cuisine has most number of 3 Ratings
  • Top revenue making restaurants receive more number of '3' ratings (Shake Shack, Blue Ribbon Sushi, Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken, RedFarm Hudson)

Question 13: The company wants to provide a promotional offer in the advertisement of the restaurants. The condition to get the offer is that the restaurants must have a rating count of more than 50 and the average rating should be greater than 4. Find the restaurants fulfilling the criteria to get the promotional offer. [3 marks]¶

In [127]:
# Write the code here
restaurant_stats = data.groupby("restaurant_name")["new_rating"].agg(["count", "mean"])
offer_fulfiling_restaurants = restaurant_stats[(restaurant_stats["count"] > 50) & (restaurant_stats["mean"] > 4)]
offer_fulfiling_restaurants

restaurant_name

Parm 68 4.5
RedFarm Broadway 59 4.474576
RedFarm Hudson 55 4.490909
Shake Shack 219 4.561644
The Meatball Shop 132 4.689394

Observations:¶

Following are the restaurants that match the criteria of 'greater than 50 ratings' & 'mean rating greater than 4.

Note : This is based on the imputed rating data

  1. Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken
  2. Blue Ribbon Sushi
  3. Parm
  4. RedFarm Broadway
  5. RedFarm Hudson
  6. Shake Shack
  7. The Meatball Shop

Question 14: The company charges the restaurant 25% on the orders having cost greater than 20 dollars and 15% on the orders having cost greater than 5 dollars. Find the net revenue generated by the company across all orders. [3 marks]¶

In [249]:
# Write the code here
orders_cost_above_20 = data[data['cost_of_the_order'] > 20]['cost_of_the_order'].sum()
print(orders_cost_above_20)
orders_cost_above_5 = data[(data['cost_of_the_order'] > 5) & (data['cost_of_the_order'] <= 20)]['cost_of_the_order'].sum()
print(orders_cost_above_5)
net_revenue = (orders_cost_above_20 * 0.25) + (orders_cost_above_5 * 0.15)
print(f"Net revenue generated by the company: ${net_revenue:.2f}")

14754.91
16517.170000000002
Net revenue generated by the company: $6166.30

Observations:¶

  • The total revenue generated by the company across all orders is $6166.30

Question 15: The company wants to analyze the total time required to deliver the food. What percentage of orders take more than 60 minutes to get delivered from the time the order is placed? (The food has to be prepared and then delivered.) [2 marks]¶

In [251]:
# Write the code here
data['total_delivery_time'] = data['food_preparation_time'] + data['delivery_time']
orders_taking_more_than_60_mins = (data['total_delivery_time'] > 60).sum()
order_percent = (orders_taking_more_than_60_mins/len(data)) * 100
print(f"Number of orders with total time > 60 mins: {order_percent:.2f}")

Number of orders with total time > 60 mins: 10.54

Observations:¶

  • Approximately 11% of orders (or 200 orders in total) take more than 60 mins to get delivered

Question 16: The company wants to analyze the delivery time of the orders on weekdays and weekends. How does the mean delivery time vary during weekdays and weekends? [2 marks]¶

In [130]:
# Write the code here
print("\nWeekend delivery time : ", data[data['day_of_the_week'] == 'Weekend']['delivery_time'].mean())
print("\nWeekday delivery time : ",data[data['day_of_the_week'] == 'Weekday']['delivery_time'].mean())

Weekend delivery time :  22.4700222057735

Weekday delivery time :  28.340036563071298

Observations:¶

  • Delivery time on weekends is quicker compared to Weekends by approximately 6 mins. On weekdays delivery takes around 28+ minutes whereas on weekends it is only around 22+ minutes

Conclusion and Recommendations¶

Question 17: What are your conclusions from the analysis? What recommendations would you like to share to help improve the business? (You can use cuisine type and feedback ratings to drive your business recommendations.) [6 marks]¶

Conclusions:¶

  1. 59% of the orders are from repeat customers placing more than 1 order

    • There are 1200 unique customers on the platform
    • There are 14 customers who have spent more than $100 on the orders
    • Average spending (i.e., cost_of_the_order) by a customer per order ranges between $16.45 to $33.32

  2. Top 5 restaurants account for 35% of the order cost or food aggregator platform transactions

    • There are 178 unique restaurants on the platform
    • Top 5 grossing restaurants are
      1. Shake Shack
      2. The Meatball Shop
      3. Blue Ribbon Sushi
      4. Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken
      5. Parm

  3. American is the most preferred cuisine.

    • Japanese, Italian & Chinese are the next.
    • Vietnamese, Spanish, Korean are least preferred
    • Average cost of the 4 top preferred cuisines is comparable and below $15
    • Average cost of Thai, French cuisines are High

  4. All (provided) ratings are equal or above 3.

    • Distribution of lower rating '3' is across cuisines and restaurants
    • American, Japanese, Italian and Chinese have received most number of ratings
    • Top revenue making restaurants receive more number of '3' ratings especially during weekends (Shake Shack, Blue Ribbon Sushi, Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken, RedFarm Hudson)

  5. Total time taken for delivering food (i.e., food preparation time and delivery time) is High on weekdays compared to weekends

    • Total time taken has no (visible) correlation to ratings, as per the provided data
    • Distribution of food preparation time is uniform across cuisines & delivery times are comparably lesser than food preparation times
    • Top selling cuisines (American, Japanese, Italian, Chinese) have comparable delivery and food preparation times
    • Vietnamese cuisine takes more time delivering than food preparation

  6. Cost range $10 to $15 has the highest number of orders

  7. Across all cuisines, significantly High number of orders are received on Weekends

  8. Average cost of American cuisine is High on weekends compared to Weekdays and for Japanese the price is similar. This is also leading to higher revenues on weekends as the order numbers are high

Recommendations:¶

  1. Increasing the number of restaurants on American, Japanese, Italian and Chinese can lead to more revenue on the platform, as they appear to be the choise of customers

    • Mexican and Indian cuisines are the 5th and 6th in revenue. They also could be increased
    • Cuisines like Vietnamese, Korean are least preferred. Not investing on adding these cuisines to the aggregator platform can be reviewed.

  2. Lower rating of 3 is seen high on weekends compared to weekends. Also the total delivery times are lesser on weekends.

    • Though the delivery times are lesser on weekends, the perceived value of the customers could be that the delivery on weekends could be even faster.
    • FoodHub can work with the delivery teams to explore options for faster delivery on weekends
    • This exercise could yield marginal gains as the time vs cost or time vs rating are not visibly seen in data. This is a perceived value
    • There could also be a quality drop in the food during weekends due to the volume or orders and this can be explored

  3. 59% of customers have made more than 1 order. They can be incentivised through coupons to improve customer relationship and stickiness to the platform

  4. Average customer spend is in the $15 range per order. Adding more options on that range across cuisines could increase the net revenue

In [ ]:
